transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hi, Maurice.
How are you?
I'm doing well, Andi.
How are you doing?
I'm good.
Nice to meet you.
Where are you calling from?
So I'm based out of Salt Lake City.
Awesome.
Yeah.
Very cool.
I'm on the East Coast, so a little later than you today.
Nice.
Cool.
pretty standard interview structure.
I can start by giving you a sense of growthx, how we're thinking about the role, kind of where this fits.
I'm guessing you've already spoken to Tucker.
Yes, I have.
Cool.
And then we'll spend most of the time talking about you.
These usually do not take 45 minutes.
We'll have time for questions at the end.
But yeah, if we end early, it's not for any, don't be nervous.
Okay, cool.
Okay, cool.
So yeah, growthx has been around for a little over a year now, and we're actually going through a reorg right now.
So it's kind of an interesting time to be joining the company.

We were operating in like a really traditional pod model, and with like directors leading a book of business, we're shifting that really separating out strategy from relationship management from like content execution is the goal of this reorg.
So we're still going to have pods where, I mean, everything that we do is built on like our own internal platform that uses AI workflows to generate content.
And so we'll have managing editors or content managers.
Generating that content day-to-day, and they will be managing like two to three clients, just doing production, like end-to-end editing, making sure pieces are published ready.
Most clients were publishing five pieces of content a week.
And then we'll have engagement managers who are going to be kind of like the account success-focused relationship owners managing eight today, but ideally 12 accounts by the end of Q1.

And they will, again, just be focused on kind of like relationship strategy, customer success, just like thinking about like performance on a day-to-day basis and delivery against kind of like the project delivery.
And then...

Directors are taking a step back from owning individual accounts to be a pooled resource, thinking mostly about strategy and, like, if we need to shift our focus for a certain client, kind of how do we come at the content strategy from a different angle?
What are the SEO opportunities?
How are we thinking about AEO for this client?

Like, helping kind of on almost, I would say, possibly more often, but at least a quarterly basis, you know, we need to think about the direction that we're going with this client, make sure that our KPIs are going in the right direction, and, like, shift the strategy, the focus, like, send us in a new direction, and then they can take a step back again.
And then if an account needs additional help, a flag, anything like that, a director can step in, but they will be a pooled resource because that's strategic.
Work requires really just like deep thought and time to do that like work.
And when directors were managing accounts alongside the engagement managers, they were just like, everybody was getting sucked into production.
We weren't, nobody was taking the step back, kind of everybody gets mired in the weeds.
So that's why we're separating things out.
That's what the director model looks like today.
So things that are going to be important in this role are, and we'll be managing that in linear.
I think we're looking for a new project management platform, like to think about that capacity, but it will be very similar to the way like engineering teams pick up tickets.
Yeah, so that's high level, but like, yeah, just like SEO strategy, like any sort of, like, experience in, like, SEO.
Ayo.
Ayo.
Ayo.
Ayo.
And kind of setting, like, direction for partnerships and helping set up, like, teams for success so that they can, like, run without you, essentially.
Like, the directors will, for the next few months, be building a lot of playbooks as they're doing the work.
Okay.
Yeah.
That makes a lot of sense.
I think that is a really smart way to go.
So, having been involved in other agencies, like, as soon as, if you have that director too far into the weeds, like, all their time is gone.
So, I think that's a good decision for you guys.
Yeah.
Hopefully.
Hopefully.
Yeah.
Well, let's talk about you.

love to get your elevator pitch here, kind of where you're coming from.

But for the most part, like, I really enjoy building out the strategy.
But I'm also very close to execution.
Again, paid programs, SEO, content, and ABM.

But that's a little bit about me and what I've been doing.
And, but again, that's why I think that my background with this seems like a really interesting opportunity and decided to, that's obviously why we're talking today.

Can you talk to me a little bit about how you think about connecting the dots on, like, pipeline, like,
And demonstrating your sort of efficacy in that space.

But we are, we exclusively do organic.
So just to clarify that.
And so how have you thought about telling that story previously?
And, you know, how might you approach it with a client?

Yeah, in terms of like the organic side, content side, if you really think about it, at least the way that I explain it internally is, if we don't have a solid content, content strategy, solid organic strategy, what are, what's the actual story that we're telling our audience?
It's the prospects, the prospects are current customers, whether you're looking at, you know, inbound or you're looking at, you know, expanding.
All of the people that you're trying to get in front of need content in order for you to tell that story.
And really, in general, like, if you don't, if you don't, if you're not top of mind of the folks that you want to get in front of, your pipeline is going to suffer.

I mean, even when you, like, you invest in your product marketing teams, you invest in your product teams, your customer teams, sales teams, but if they don't have a consistent story to tell, people are going, I mean, some people will find you.
I mean, a lot of these companies are big for a reason, but, you know, if you're not creating a story, a narrative, organically, especially, it's, everything's going to fall through the cracks.

And so that's, when you start there and basically establish that content really is king, and if you're not going to be found organically, you know, other than, you're going to have to invest, you know, three, four, five X in your sales team.
it's, it's, it's, it's, it's, it's, it's,
They're going to be calling and calling and calling.
If you can really generate that inbound engine, everything flows better.

I mean, if you have companies who, if you look at companies who are like, oh, we have 85% of our traffic coming inbound versus companies that are saying 85% of my traffic, my deals are coming out, coming from outbound, from sales teams.
The people on the other side are struggling a lot more, and the other teams are typically growing.
So I think for me, like, I have to have that initial foundation.
But once you're able to say, hey, these are the content things that are in place, you have, I don't want to get too lost in the weeds in terms of marketing attribution.
But, like, you see people coming through content pieces from, you're looking at your organic traffic to see if it's coming from social, from, from all these different places.

So once you're able to say, all right, this is working versus this isn't, you're able to invest, you're able to experiment, and really that's where growth is fueled.

mean, so we're, just to clarify a little bit more, we are organic, like through like SEO blog content, really, like exclusively.

Once you're able to see where this traffic is coming from, and you're being shown that these long tail keywords are showing up, that's where you're really building that inbound engine.

It's a little bit easier.
But once you're invested and you're creating consistent content, you're actually making noise.
They're going to pick you up.
And that's where that inbound organic motion is going to come from.

And so, essentially, I created a framework of content repurposing through, like, AI workflows.

And we essentially created at least five different pieces of content from this one piece.
We would like, short-form videos.
Maybe they're, like, short to long-form, so, like, five to ten minutes.

We would create blog posts that came.

We would plug in a bunch of the things that they

used another tool called Opus Pro.

mean, we ran into that with my, in my last role a lot because it was an AI product that I, that we were marketing.

can say, I can sound like I'm technical in, in terms of like developers and folks.

And obviously, there, I was able to jump into the weeds and really find out if certain things were correct, because we had some technical folks, but I think that's really what you have to do is make sure you have as much content as you can to feed the engine.

And then, you know, making sure that we're hitting those deadlines, making sure that we're being very communicative of the, of how things are working, how things are moving through the funnel.
